speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter gave me that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four; March 15th--

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what?

speaker: interviewer
text: --1995; Sara Hertzberg. You're wearing a locket with--

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanna open it.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll talk about that. But you were going to talk about how your family survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Survived, OK. So mine brother, Usher, the guy sure killed. My other brother, with the family, they found them. They went in with forks. And they start doing that, and they start yelling. So they took him out--

speaker: interviewer
text: Little pitchforks into the hay.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes-- yes. And they killed them. They shot them all-- the three kids, and my oldest brother, with his wife. And the rest of them went-- no-- no, just a minute. Oh, they went like the partisan, but didn't know-- like it was a small bush and a [YIDDISH] we call it in Yiddish, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Forest, yeah. So when they came to the partisan, the-- the goyish partisan, the-- the Russian, the-- the-- the-- they took everything away from them, then led them where-- I don't know how they got clothes, what did they did with themself. But they were living like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they found a place in-- where you take off corn. You make this-- before the corn, you know, you put them up like that. Do you ever seen that? And a dry-- so they were hidden in there. In the sheaves.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the sheaf. So mine-- Chavche was in one. And she was in-- what's his name? Chanale was in another one. Chanale was with Chavche, and they-- and Freidke, Fela, had Rivche on her hand. And my brother said that he's gonna go in the morning. And he's gonna go at-- it was at night-- to the city. Maybe he's gonna find some food. So my sister, Freidke, says, I'll go with you. That was Chiel who was going. And-- but it was at that time the dogs were so trained that when they felt a Jew coming to a village or to a city, all the dogs start talking. And then they all came out, the goyim, because they knew. They smelled Jewish people. I can't understand how that happened even. And as they were wandering around, both-- just arrived to the city-- the guy who was our neighbor, his kids was brought up together with ours. My father used to-- Friday, bring them fish and the first challah before we made the moitzi. And we were so close. This guy spotted them-- the two of them-- and with other-- with other people, with the kids. They took him into his place. And he's got a place in the-- for chickens. And that place was like a thicket. It was-- the roof was there, but it was a-- not a-- artificial, not a strong one. He took them both in there. And they were crying. They knew it. And he had a-- a night light. You know, we used to have these--

speaker: interviewer
text: A lantern?

speaker: interviewee
text: --a lanterna, they used to call it. A--

speaker: interviewer
text: A lantern.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he kept them there both. And they were sitting both in the corner. They knew they going to the dead. They, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So he was not keeping them to save them.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was keeping them to give them away in the morning to the-- to the German. And they were crying and begging them. But God helped. 4 o'clock in the morning, it came a rain, a terrible rain outside. And it was clapping. And that-- that ceiling was like tin or something-- so bang, bang, bang. You can hear the thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: This guy fell asleep. And they try what to do. One was watching the other. And, you know, you know, the guy?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they picked up that ceiling. First, my-- my sister, she was like that. And she slide out like a-- like a-- like a cat; and then my brother. And when they slide out from there, they start running. And they start running till-- and then, my sister and-- and the cousin, Chanale, came across, because they thought they are killed. It was very, very-- it was about maybe 6 o'clock in the morning. It was still were dark. And they were very happy that they are all together. And they were sitting still in these things, this--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were still in the field.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were still in the field. But then the war was going on. And the German was shooting this way. And the-- and the Russian were coming this way. And my sister, Freidke was holding--

speaker: interviewer
text: Rivkale.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Rivka on the hands. And they shot through her. And she was shot in here. And this one was shot maybe in the heart. And then-- and a minute later she sees she's dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: In her arms?

speaker: interviewee
text: In her arms, yeah. She-- they killed her in her arms. And Chanale was sitting with my other sister and with my brother. And there was a terrible shooting there. So Chanale got shot right through-- the doctor. Where she's in-- over there. And my other sister too, Chavche, they have a lot of these things. They didn't take never them out. They're still walking around them-- and my brother too. But the Russian came. The Russian came. And they came out. When they came out-- so my brother was then, I don't know, 18, 19 then.

speaker: interviewer
text: They ask him, [NON-ENGLISH]? How old are you? It means a grandfather, how old are you? You know, grown with a beard and looked terrible, [NON-ENGLISH] and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they-- they came out and they were sick. They were-- they were dragging themselves. They were bleeding from the-- from the, you know, from the shrapnel with everything. And so they-- they took him, I think, someplace to a hospital. They start curing them. And-- but before that, they had to bury Rivka. So they all went. There was a little-- I remember, it was close where we owned our-- our field. It was a-- a very young--

speaker: interviewer
text: Forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Forest, yeah. I used to go when I was a kid there-- pick blueberries, and-- and mushrooms, and-- in that place. They digged a hole in there, and they buried her in there. And the Russian took them away to a hospital. And they helped them. And later, from there on, I guess when they felt better, they let a memory-- gave to us-- Chanale went to school. She went to go for a-- she wanted to be a doctor. And the bracha helped them also. The bracha was very brave then and it was a lot of people from our own already. And it was, you know, it was going on. Rabbi [NON-ENGLISH] and all the other people came already to bring the people. So there was-- it was going on-- a pictures all the time-- like you see somethings on television. There, they didn't have that. And people came. They broke down. They didn't meet anybody. They came to look for parents. There was a goy living. And they were afraid they-- after that, they make right away a big pogrom in Kielce. And when we came-- and people want to go to Kielce to see if anybody's alive. It was-- so they made a big pogrom. They killed a lot, a lot of Jews at that time, when they came back already. And the Polacks and all, they say, oh. We thought that no one came back. Look at how many they-- look at how many people what came back. And they still alive, and they're here. And my family went to Israel. And we went to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: I wonder if I could ask you some-- some questions. What-- what do you think enabled you to survive? What made it possible for you to survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that a person should never give up hope. And hope is if you believe in God. Because this is the thing which you hold onto. If you drown, if you do anything-- so God or mom-- mother. So the hope that there is a God, which a lot of people didn't believe anymore. And that after, they weren't special, they were in concentrations. And when the German took them together, and they tell them to dance, and they made a fire all around. And they were sitting, like you go Maple Leaf Garden, someplace on these high seats.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bleachers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And that was their enjoyment. They enjoy seeing the way people got burnt, and the way the-- what they did to people, that was, to them, a show. That was to them to go-- like you-- like you pay a lot of money to go to see the showboat or something else, something, you know. That was constantly-- constantly going on. And if you were a strong person-- I mean, I was, and I am. But even today, when my husband passed away, when I walk out on the house, I said, I am still rich-- which I am not. I have a home. I don't have to claim to anybody for a dollar. Thanks God my health is not bad. I can help other people. I-- I go-- you know, secretly, I cook for people what they alone in apartments. And I have people where they played piano for me and-- and other people. I have a lot to give. I'm very lonely now that I cannot get involved, really, with other people to do those things, because that gives me a great pleasure. I-- money wouldn't be to me so-- so much worth it that I-- if I go and I-- I visit somebody, I can talk to them. When I went out to sing in the bakeries for the first time, I thought, I'm not gonna make it, because I cried when I see people are sitting sick. People, what I saw, they used to come to my store and shop, and now they are like that. Or they don't know what you're talking to them. Or they smile to me, but they in a bad condition. I couldn't. And they told me, you're doing a mitzvah. You be strong. You're singing for them. You're bringing them to life. And I went down with my microphone, and I give them to sing. And I-- you know. For years since-- I started, I think in '70 something. The last one was when my husband took sick in '89 or something, '87.

speaker: interviewer
text: So singing has been a very important part of your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Singing and going to the shul, that's my life. Shabbas, when I go to the shul, I feel I'm a Jew, and I enjoy every minute. I go to different shuls, because I like to hear different chazanim. And anything, some Jewish concerts or whatever is going on in Jewish life, I am so proud to be there. And I always go back, look at how many chances I had. I wouldn't have been here. I must be very special that God let me live. And I-- and I can tell my story now. And I wish-- my father was always proud of me. Because I was very good in school. They used-- used to give me spending money. And I used to save all this money-- put it away in a-- there was a [NON-ENGLISH] we call it. It was a-- a bank, like in a bank. And [NON-ENGLISH] was hospitalization-- what was another name for a bank-- [NON-ENGLISH]. And I put every money. When one time they were very broke, and they didn't have money. And my father used-- had to buy a horse to go and do the business. And he didn't have. And it was like Yom Kippur in the house. Everybody was crying. The horse died, and we can't live. He can't go and handle. And I went to my-- my father. And I picked up the book. And I give him here that it's 25 zlotys I saved up-- all the money he used to give me for this, and for this, and for that. And I saved every cent. You know, he didn't know what to do with me, because I saved the family. He went out. He bought a horse. And everything started going back. And there's a lot of times that-- that I use my brains. And I always go back that I drive a car. I said, my God, if my mother would get up or my father, see me driving a car, they'd probably die. You know, so I always give myself a lot of credit for everything. I went to get my license-- driver's license. And I didn't know if I'll make it. And my husband says, ho, [NON-ENGLISH] after the [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what the [NON-ENGLISH] is. Say a prayer if somebody dies. Say a prayer-- this [NON-ENGLISH] in Yiddish-- that-- that-- that you tried but nothing came out. You're not gonna make it. I said look. I'm gonna go. A girlfriend's she got it. And I said, if she's got, I will get it too. I said, I'm gonna go. I'm not going for an emergency. But I'll go. And if I'll get it, it's gonna be-- I'm gonna be proud of it. But I have to try. And I was 42-years-old. And I said, I'm gonna take 42 lessons-- how old I am-- and not one more. And if I don't make it, I'm not. And I made it in 22. Because I didn't understand the language. But the inspector asked me questions. And I-- I told them, in my-- my-- in-- you know, and from that he gave me the license. Some of them I knew, the rest I didn't know. I didn't know the English.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I'm proud always of myself. And whatever I do, a little thing, I give myself credit. Things go bad, I-- I do something good for somebody else. And I said look. I am healthy, and I can do it. What does this people go? Sometimes I get very depressed, you know, since my husband died. I go to the Y, and I look at the other people. There are so many people alone. They do the same as me. They do exercise. They sit, play cards. They do. I said, I do better things. I go out. I sing. I'm involved in Beth David Synagogue. I'm involved in-- in-- Adas Israel, they called me for-- for a-- [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to see- we're going to see pictures of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I sing in other choirs, with [NON-ENGLISH] with Hadassah Choir, with [NON-ENGLISH]. I'm climbing from one place to the other.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you one more thing. It's just really a-- a last question.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one time.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we'll look at some pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what made you decide to come forward and make this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A lot of things. I wanted to write a book. Not because I want to make money, but I thought my life is very interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when my husband died and I was very low on gas, they send me to the Toronto General Hospital. And there was a Jewish woman also. She's a-- just a social worker. And I was talking to her.

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't give me any help, or tell me what to do, or where to go. And I really got mad. I said, if I go to my doctor, he gives me a prescription for a medicine. I'm talking to you an hour and a 1/2, and you didn't tell me anything what to do. I told her all kind of-- And she said, she want to come. She listened to my whole story-- what I said now. I told her. She said, I want to write a book. She came in here. She looked at all my pictures. She said, it's very interesting and everything. But I told her later, later. I wasn't in the mood.

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Cause, you know, I-- I went back. Why did I lose my husband? How bad it is-- I never understand what it means to being alone. And it's completely-- everything changed. It just like-- like a branch you cut off. We never separated.

speaker: interviewee
text: I make a joke now. I say, my marriage, when I got married, I paid three ruble. And we lived happily 50 years, with it was in the year we made the bar mitzvah of my grandson. And he was still there. That was 50 years. We're supposed to make a big party for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I decided, you know, when she came, she said she wants to make a book. I said, it's very interesting. When I went to Israel, my niece made the tape. And I was talking. She wanted to know how her mother looked. How-- her mother couldn't remember everything-- and everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she made a tape, and I kept on telling. And this woman got so interested. But, you know, as time was going on, and I was not good, I said, I'll call you. And I never called her.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I heard that people making tapes-- but everybody told me, if you weren't in Germany, you weren't sitting hidden, and you weren't in the concentration, you don't get the video [NON-ENGLISH], you're not-- you're not a survivor. You-- you're not in this category.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wait and wait. You know, it was killing me. It was burning me up. I wanted so much to be-- I suffered so much. I went through so much. I have so much to tell. I have so much to-- to give. So much-- I consider myself a good person. I-- I consider myself that I like to help. I-- I like to do good things for people. And-- and why shouldn't I have the right to be also any place? In those places, I have friends, you know, where they were hiding by goyim. They weren't even in concentration. They were hiding by goyim. They lived through terrible. They were under the Irish papers, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they made tapes. And they have pictures. And they have a video and everything. And I was so jealous. So I kept on nagging myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I learned all the papers. And I phoned up once the Lipa Green Building. And I didn't get a good answer. But the last time I think I took it out from the And I said, I have to talk to somebody. And I think I spoke to Joanne? She answered the phone. Is it-- To Janet.

speaker: interviewee
text: Janet-- Janet. She said, of course you have the right. [GASPS] Janet, I said, you made my day. I was so miserable that day, because I kept on going to my husband on the cemetery, and I come back. And I think like it just happened. I can't go to the Mount Sinai, because he died in the general. So wherever I go, I come back, and I'm there. Then I go to a funeral. I can't. I can't look at the casket. And I am so broken up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wanted to tell you what made a joke. I said, you know, because people don't like you when you cry. And I said like that, look. I married for three ruble, and it last 50 years. Now kids pay $50,000 for their marriage, and sometimes it lasts three months or three years. So I am good up.

speaker: interviewee
text: My marriage was strong. It doesn't mean if you make a marry-- if you marry for $50,000 or I married for three ruble, where it's worth here $0.10 probably. I said-- and I was very happy. And I appreciate every minute and everything in all my life what I did. I just miss my husband. He went too fast for me. And other than that, you know, I still join the organization. I'm still going on. And I'm-- I still proud of myself that I'm Jewish no matter what. I'll never change. And when we made the third seder, I used to sing [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, [NON-ENGLISH]. But when it came to the [NON-ENGLISH], it says-- and if they find you, you a Jew. I really let them know. [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know, I'll die with God, with pride that I am a Jew, that I-- that I-- you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry, maybe--

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll see some pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. You mean we-- From the left is my sister, Chavche. Next to her is my brother, Chiel. And next to him is Freidke, with her husband, Bennick.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken before the war. I don't remember exactly which year. But it must be maybe 1938. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] This picture was taken in 1938, before the war, in city, Wegrow, where I was born. And on the top is my brother, Usher. And next to him is Rivka, his girlfriend. And next is my brother, Chiel. And next we have Chavche. It's a funny story about that picture, because she used to live not far from the cemetery where my father was buried. And I used to go from far away, watch them-- watch the cemetery where my father was buried. And she saw me. She called me into her house. And we became friends. And then I introduced my brother. And they fell in love. And she was a very wonderful girl, and would have been a [NON-ENGLISH] if it wouldn't be the war. And I remember the song, what she was singing to him. [NON-ENGLISH SINGING] That's it. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And here is my sister, Freidke, before the war. And next to her is my brother, Usher, who got killed by a good friend of him, a Polish guy, just about two, three weeks before the war was finished. And he gave him away to the German. He-- he caught him in the field, and he gave him away to the German for some sugar and some butter-- whatever he got for it. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And here is my brother, Yechiel, when he got his diploma that he is a rabbi. He came back from Siedlce to Wegrow to see us all, because-- and that's me, Sara. And we took a picture. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture when I finished grade seven-- school. And we went to Warsaw to see where Pilsudski used to live. Because that year, Pilsudski died. That was the president of Poland. He was very good to all the Jewish people. And I am here, the fourth from the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: In which row?

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- second row.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the second row. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That's me with a white scarf. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] And that's my brother, Chiel, before the war. When he finished for rabbi, he came to see us, to Wegrow. And next to them is my cousin, Gittel, with her husband, which they kept me in Bialystok in the wartime. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That picture is my husband. He's right on the-- on the right side, on the top of the picture. The next to him is his sister, Pessel, and his brother Alter. And I think that must be Esther. And that's his parents. And the other two kids, I-- I don't remember the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the picture was taken where?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken before the war, and it was sent here to his sister in Canada. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This picture is all my friends before the war. It was taken in Wegrow. I am on the bottom line from the left side. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This picture was taken when we came after the war from Russia. We came to Wroclaw. From the left is my brother and mine cousin, Chanale, the doctor, my brother, Chiel, and cousin, Chanale. And then it's my husband on the top, Leo. And it's me in the middle, Sara. And Rivka was my little daughter. And to the right is Chavche. That's the-- I met them after the war when I came to-- the first time after-- in Wroclaw. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] On the left is my auntie from Malkin. I went to see them before the-- before the war, 1939-- and her husband, and their three children. And in the next picture, I am there. And my cousin is from the right, the second guy. We took pictures, because I was leaving, and-- for school, to Bialystok, and I wanted to see them. But I never saw them anymore-- never. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That picture was taken right after the war, in Russia, in 1945, I suppose-- by Samarkand.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that's me and my husband, Leo, and my daughter, Rivka. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] OK. That's my daughter, Rivkale, born in Linz, in [NON-ENGLISH], when she was, I don't know, a year old, or a year and a 1/2 or something like that. I forget it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that picture was taken when we came to Canada. It was taken in Toronto, 1949. That's-- to the left is my sister-in-law, Fanny. And the middle is my daughter, Rivkale, 2 and a 1/2 years old. And to the right, it's me, Sara. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That picture is a sweet 16 of my daughter, Rennie. When she was in Russia, and she was small, she was very sick. And I-- I didn't think I will bring her back. I promised myself that I'll make her a very beautiful party if there will a occasion.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there she is on the left side-- is Rennie. It's her sweet 16 in the [INAUDIBLE] shul, in Toronto. And I am in the middle. And my husband, Leo, is next to me. And next to her is my younger daughter, Gildale.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That picture is the wedding of my younger daughter, Gildale, in 1970. And my husband is right beside her. You should see how proud he is of her. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] That's the picture of-- of my grandson's bar mitzvah, Sean. He's right in the middle with the tallis. From the left it's me, Sara. Next to me is my husband, Leo, and my daughter, Gilda. And from the right side, Suzanne, my granddaughter, and my son-in-law, David. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That picture is also from Sean's bar mitzvah. And you see here from the right is-- oy vey-- my son-in-law's son. Sean is next to him, and then is Shelly, and then is Rennie. Shelly is Rennie's husband. And Lorny is their son.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's it. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's the Beth Emeth Choir under direction of Charles Heller, and the cantor, [NON-ENGLISH], and Vera Shevchenko, the pianist. And I am in the first row from the left, the fourth.
